message:
feat(wal): introduce per-core buffer architecture for parallel WAL processing

This commit introduces a foundational per-core WAL buffer architecture to enable
safe parallel WAL processing across CPU cores. Key changes:

- New per_core_buffer.rs module: Implements core-local WAL buffer management with
  lock-free synchronization for cross-core updates and snapshot consistency
- Per-core WAL buffer architecture design doc: Comprehensive specification covering
  design rationale, thread safety guarantees, and integration points
- Critical invariants documentation: Establishes foundational constraints for
  concurrent buffer operations, orphan slot handling, and checkpoint guarantees
- E2E scenario catalog expansion: Extends catalog with parallel WAL buffer scenarios
- Cross-process verification script hardening: Enhanced bd-2g5.5 harness with
  improved crash scenario tracking and orphan slot reclamation validation
- Parallel WAL buffer pilot E2E test: New bd_ncivz_1 test for validating parallel
  buffer operations under multi-process crash conditions

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-n0g4q.6","title":"E2E test: corrupt random pages, verify automatic repair","description":"End-to-end integration test that validates the complete RaptorQ self-healing pipeline: write data -> generate repair symbols -> corrupt pages -> detect corruption -> repair -> verify integrity.

2. Multi-page corruption: corrupt 5% of pages randomly, verify all repaired
3. Contiguous corruption: corrupt a contiguous range of pages (simulating sector failure)
4. WAL corruption: corrupt WAL frames, verify repair from snapshot symbols
5. Snapshot corruption: corrupt snapshot pages, verify repair from WAL symbols
6. Double corruption: corrupt both WAL and snapshot for same page (should fail gracefully)
7. Corruption during write: inject corruption while writes are in-flight

- Populate database with known data (TPC-H SF0.01 or custom dataset)
- Generate repair symbols (wait for async encoding to complete)
- Inject corruption using direct file I/O (bypass database layer)

- Verify: data reads succeed and return correct values
- Verify: evidence ledger contains repair records
- Verify: BLAKE3 witness proofs are valid

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 7 scenarios pass
- [ ] Repair success rate matches theoretical capacity (corruption <= repair symbol ratio)
- [ ] Evidence ledger entries verified for each repair

- [ ] Graceful error for corruption beyond repair capacity

 {"id":"bd-n3p4","title":"Corruption Models: Define realistic corruption modes + safety rails","description":"## Goal
Define the corruption modes we will simulate and how to keep them safe/reproducible.

- **Bit flips**: flip N random bits/bytes (seeded).
- **Page zeroing**: overwrite a full page-aligned 4KB chunk with zeros.
- **Truncation**: truncate file by M bytes / to page boundary.
- **WAL tail loss** (if WAL present): truncate `-wal` after K frames.

## Safety Rails
- Operate only on `work/` copies.
- Always record seed + offsets mutated.
- Keep pre-corruption copy to compare.

 {"id":"bd-ncivz","title":"Parallel WAL — Silo/Aether Style (§15.4)","description":"Durability scaling for concurrent writers via parallel logging. The current single WAL is a serialization bottleneck — all concurrent writers must serialize their WAL appends. Score 5.3 in the opportunity matrix. Critical for the concurrent-writer story that page-level MVCC enables.

## Background
SQLite's WAL uses a single-writer model (WAL_WRITE_LOCK). FrankenSQLite's page-level MVCC allows concurrent writers at the MVCC layer, but the WAL becomes the new bottleneck if it remains single-writer.

## Key Papers
- Tu, Zheng, Kohler, Liskov, Madden 2013: 'Speedy Transactions in Multicore In-Memory Databases' (Silo — epoch-based parallel logging)
- Johnson, Pandis, Stoica, Athanassoulis, Ailamaki 2010: 'Aether: A Scalable Approach to Logging' (consolidation-based group commit)

## Architecture
Silo-style parallel logging:
1. Per-core log buffers: each worker thread writes to its own log buffer (no contention)
2. Epoch-based ordering: global epoch advances periodically. All log records within an epoch are equivalent for ordering purposes.
3. Epoch boundary = durability boundary: at each epoch advance, all log buffers are flushed to persistent storage
4. Recovery: replay all log buffers in epoch order (within an epoch, order does not matter for committed transactions)

Aether-style group commit with consolidation:
1. Writers add their log records to a consolidation buffer
2. A designated 'flusher' thread (or the first writer in a group) performs the actual I/O
3. All writers in the group are notified when the flush completes
4. Amortizes fsync overhead across multiple transactions

Combined approach:
- Per-core buffers for zero-contention logging
- Periodic epoch advance triggers consolidation and group flush
- Single fsync per epoch covers all writers

## Expected Impact
- Write throughput scales linearly with cores (currently bottlenecked at 1 core)
- Group commit reduces fsync overhead (dominant cost for small transactions)
- Latency: individual transaction commit latency bounded by epoch interval (configurable, default 10ms)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Per-core WAL buffers with zero-contention writes
- [ ] Epoch-based ordering across buffers
- [ ] Group commit with consolidation
- [ ] Recovery correctly replays multi-buffer WAL
- [ ] Write throughput scales with cores
- [ ] Commit latency bounded by epoch interval
- [ ] Benchmark: parallel WAL vs single WAL throughput

## Success Criteria

 {"id":"bd-ncivz.2","title":"Implement epoch-based ordering across per-core buffers","description":"Implement the epoch-based ordering mechanism that provides a total order across per-core WAL buffers. This is the key insight from Silo: within an epoch, the order of operations across cores does not matter for correctness.

Epoch management:
1. Global epoch counter: atomic u64, advanced periodically (configurable interval, default 10ms)
2. Epoch advance protocol: 
   a. Increment global epoch E_new = E_old + 1
   b. Wait for all active transactions to observe E_new (fence)
   c. All log records written before the fence are tagged with E_old
   d. Log records after the fence are tagged with E_new
3. Durability guarantee: when epoch E is flushed, all transactions that committed in epoch <= E are durable

Transaction commit protocol:
1. Writer seals log records in per-core buffer with current epoch
2. Writer waits for its epoch to be flushed (group commit)
3. Epoch flush: collect all sealed records from all per-core buffers for epoch E, write to persistent storage, fsync
4. Notify all waiting writers that epoch E is durable

Recovery ordering:
- Replay log records in epoch order (ascending)
- Within an epoch: apply all committed transactions (order does not matter due to MVCC isolation)
- Uncommitted transactions within an epoch: discard during recovery

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Global epoch counter with periodic advance
- [ ] Log records correctly tagged with epoch
- [ ] Group commit: flush all per-core buffers per epoch
- [ ] Writers correctly block until their epoch is durable
- [ ] Recovery replays in epoch order
- [ ] Correctness: concurrent transactions committed in different epochs maintain serial order
- [ ] Fence correctness: no log records straddling epoch boundaries","acceptance_criteria":"1.  {"id":"bd-ncivz.3","title":"Implement group commit with consolidation","description":"Implement Aether-style group commit with consolidation to amortize fsync overhead across multiple transactions. fsync is the dominant cost for small transaction commits (1-10ms per call). Group commit batches multiple transactions into a single fsync.

Consolidation protocol:
1. Writers add their sealed log records to a consolidation queue
2. Consolidation phases: FILLING (accepting records) -> FLUSHING (writing to disk) -> COMPLETE (notify waiters)
3. The first writer to enter FILLING state becomes the 'flusher'
4. Subsequent writers add records and wait on a condition variable
5. Flusher: when epoch advance triggers, collect all records in FILLING state
6. Flusher: write consolidated batch to WAL file, fsync
7. Flusher: notify all waiting writers, transition to COMPLETE
8. Next epoch begins a new FILLING phase

I/O optimization:
- Consolidated write: single writev() syscall with scatter-gather of all per-core buffers
- io_uring for async write+fsync on Linux (with fallback to synchronous)
- Direct I/O to bypass page cache (WAL is write-once, page cache is wasted)

Tuning:
- Group size target: configurable (default: wait up to 1ms for additional writers before flushing)
- Maximum group delay: bounded to ensure tail latency (default: 10ms)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Consolidation protocol correctly batches multiple writers
- [ ] Single fsync per group commit
- [ ] writev() scatter-gather for efficient I/O
- [ ] io_uring integration (with fallback)
- [ ] Commit latency bounded by max group delay
- [ ] Benchmark: commits/second with group commit vs individual fsync

+# Per-Core WAL Buffer Architecture (`bd-ncivz.1`)
+
+## Purpose
+
+Define the design contract for per-core WAL buffering used by the parallel WAL path.  
+This artifact provides:
+
+- data model and state machine
+- explicit invariants and failure modes
+- deterministic fallback behavior
+- MVCC integration points
+- prototype evidence links (unit + e2e)
+
+## Scope
+
+This bead is a design-and-prototype slice. It does **not** replace the existing commit path end-to-end yet.  
+It establishes a concrete contract that `bd-ncivz.2+` can implement against.
+
+## Core Model
+
+Each writer maps to a **core-local WAL buffer**.  
+A core-local buffer is double-buffered:
+
+- `active` lane: writable by the mapped writer
+- `flush` lane: immutable while a flush is in progress
+- `overflow` queue: bounded spillover when `active` fills before epoch advance
+
+Default sizing:
+
+- `active` capacity: `4 MiB` per core
+- fallback trigger: `8 MiB` queued overflow per core
+
+## WAL Record Schema
+
+Each buffered mutation record carries:
+
+- `txn_token` (`txn_id`, `txn_epoch`)
+- `epoch` (group-commit epoch tag)
+- `page_id`
+- `begin_seq`
+- `end_seq` (optional until commit sealing)
+- `before_image`
+- `after_image`
+
+The prototype uses this shape directly in `crates/fsqlite-wal/src/per_core_buffer.rs`.
+
+## Thread-to-Buffer Mapping
+
+Deterministic mapping:
+
+- writer thread is assigned one logical core id for the transaction lifetime
+- all record appends for that writer go to that core’s `active` lane
+- no cross-core append sharing on the fast path
+
+Prototype evidence checks this with one writer thread per core and lock-contention counter assertions.
+
+## NUMA Strategy
+
+Allocation policy:
+
+- allocate each core buffer on startup
+- place memory on the local NUMA node of the mapped core whenever topology info is available
+- if NUMA placement fails/unavailable, fall back to standard allocation and keep semantics unchanged
+
+`bd-ncivz.1` captures this as a contract; low-level NUMA placement hooks are planned for implementation beads.
+
+## State Machine
+
+Lane states:
+
+- `Writable`
+- `Sealed { epoch }`
+- `Flushing { epoch }`
+
+Transitions:
+
+1. `Writable -> Sealed` on epoch seal (`seal_active`)
+2. `Sealed -> Flushing` by lane rotation (`begin_flush`)
+3. `Flushing -> Writable` after successful drain (`complete_flush`)
+4. `Writable -> Writable` normal append
+5. `Writable -> overflow queue` when lane full and overflow policy permits
+
+Invalid transitions are rejected deterministically (error return, no partial mutation).
+
+## Overflow and Deterministic Fallback
+
+Policies:
+
+- `BlockWriter`: return blocked outcome on active-lane overflow
+- `AllocateOverflow`: queue record in overflow queue
+
+Deterministic fallback trigger:
+
+- if overflow bytes exceed `overflow_fallback_bytes`, latch `ForceSerializedDrain`
+- fallback action drains `active + flush + overflow` through serialized path and resets lanes
+
+This avoids unbounded queue growth and ensures a predictable failure containment path.
+
+## MVCC and Commit Integration Points
+
+Write path integration points:
+
+1. MVCC page write emits `WalRecord` into mapped core buffer
+2. transaction commit marks records sealed under current epoch
+3. group flush drains sealed lanes and hands batches to WAL append/fsync path
+4. on fallback trigger, coordinator drains through serialized compatibility-safe path
+
+This design preserves the project requirement that concurrent-writer mode remains enabled by default.
+
+## Invariants
+
+`INV-NCIVZ-1` Single-writer per core lane:
+- one mapped writer owns one `active` lane at a time
+
+`INV-NCIVZ-2` Lane exclusivity:
+- a lane cannot be `Writable` and `Flushing` simultaneously
+
+`INV-NCIVZ-3` Epoch monotonicity:
+- `epoch` tags on sealed/flush batches are non-decreasing per core
+
+`INV-NCIVZ-4` Flush immutability:
+- records in `Flushing` lane are immutable until flush completion
+
+`INV-NCIVZ-5` Overflow boundedness:
+- overflow growth beyond threshold always latches deterministic fallback
+
+`INV-NCIVZ-6` No silent drop:
+- appends are either accepted in active lane, queued overflow, or explicitly blocked
+
+`INV-NCIVZ-7` Deterministic fallback reset:
+- serialized drain leaves both lanes writable/empty and clears fallback latch
+
+`INV-NCIVZ-8` No-contention target:
+- one writer per core has zero observed lock-contention events in prototype run
+
+## Failure Modes and Handling
+
+1. Active lane overflow before epoch seal:
+- handled by configured policy (`BlockWriter` or `AllocateOverflow`)
+
+2. Overflow runaway:
+- deterministic `ForceSerializedDrain` trigger and drain
+
+3. Invalid state transitions:
+- rejected with explicit error, no state corruption
+
+4. Flush failure:
+- keep lane in flushing state and surface error; no writer success is reported from failed flush
+
+5. Mapping mismatch (invalid core id):
+- hard error at dispatch boundary; no implicit remap
+
+## Observability Contract (Required Fields)
+
+Per flush/fallback event must include:
+
+- `trace_id`
+- `run_id`
+- `scenario_id`
+- `bead_id`
+- `core_id`
+- `epoch`
+- `lane_state_from`
+- `lane_state_to`
+- `outcome`
+- `duration_us`
+- `error_code` (nullable)
+
+The e2e verifier emits these in JSONL summary artifacts.
+
+## Prototype Evidence
+
+Unit tests:
+
+- `bd_ncivz_1_state_machine_double_buffering`
+- `bd_ncivz_1_overflow_block_writer_policy`
+- `bd_ncivz_1_overflow_allocate_triggers_deterministic_fallback`
+- `bd_ncivz_1_per_core_pool_concurrent_writers_no_contention`
+
+All are implemented in:
+
+- `crates/fsqlite-wal/src/per_core_buffer.rs`
+
+Pilot e2e script:
+
+- `e2e/bd_ncivz_1_parallel_wal_buffer_pilot.sh`
+
+Artifacts:
+
+- `artifacts/ncivz_1_parallel_wal_buffer/`
+
+Replay command:
+
+- `RUST_TEST_THREADS=1 rch exec -- cargo test -p fsqlite-wal bd_ncivz_1_ -- --nocapture`

+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+set -euo pipefail
+
+BEAD_ID="bd-ncivz.1"
+SCENARIO_ID="E2E-CNC-007"
+SEED=2026021601
+WORKSPACE_ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/.." && pwd)"
+REPORT_ROOT="${WORKSPACE_ROOT}/artifacts/ncivz_1_parallel_wal_buffer"
+RUN_ID="${BEAD_ID}-$(date -u +%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ)-$$"
+TRACE_ID="${RUN_ID}"
+REPORT_JSONL="${REPORT_ROOT}/${RUN_ID}.jsonl"
+LOG_PATH="${REPORT_ROOT}/${RUN_ID}.log"
+TEST_FILTER="bd_ncivz_1_"
+REPLAY_COMMAND="RUST_TEST_THREADS=1 rch exec -- cargo test -p fsqlite-wal ${TEST_FILTER} -- --nocapture"
+JSON_OUTPUT=false
+
+if [[ "${1:-}" == "--json" ]]; then
+    JSON_OUTPUT=true
+fi
+
+if ! command -v rch >/dev/null 2>&1; then
+    printf 'bead_id=%s level=ERROR run_id=%s trace_id=%s scenario_id=%s phase=preflight error_code=E_RCH_MISSING\n' \
+        "${BEAD_ID}" "${RUN_ID}" "${TRACE_ID}" "${SCENARIO_ID}" >&2
+    exit 2
+fi
+
+mkdir -p "${REPORT_ROOT}"
+: >"${REPORT_JSONL}"
+
+emit_event() {
+    local phase="$1"
+    local event_type="$2"
+    local outcome="$3"
+    local duration_us="$4"
+    local error_code="$5"
+    local schema_conforms="$6"
+
+    jq -nc \
+        --arg trace_id "${TRACE_ID}" \
+        --arg run_id "${RUN_ID}" \
+        --arg scenario_id "${SCENARIO_ID}" \
+        --arg bead_id "${BEAD_ID}" \
+        --arg phase "${phase}" \
+        --arg event_type "${event_type}" \
+        --arg outcome "${outcome}" \
+        --arg error_code "${error_code}" \
+        --arg replay_command "${REPLAY_COMMAND}" \
+        --arg log_path "${LOG_PATH}" \
+        --arg report_jsonl "${REPORT_JSONL}" \
+        --argjson duration_us "${duration_us}" \
+        --argjson seed "${SEED}" \
+        --argjson schema_conforms "${schema_conforms}" \
+        '{
+            trace_id: $trace_id,
+            run_id: $run_id,
+            scenario_id: $scenario_id,
+            bead_id: $bead_id,
+            phase: $phase,
+            event_type: $event_type,
+            core_id: null,
+            epoch: null,
+            lane_state_from: null,
+            lane_state_to: null,
+            outcome: $outcome,
+            duration_us: $duration_us,
+            error_code: (if $error_code == "null" then null else $error_code end),
+            seed: $seed,
+            schema_conforms: $schema_conforms,
+            replay_command: $replay_command,
+            artifact_paths: [$log_path, $report_jsonl]
+        }' >>"${REPORT_JSONL}"
+}
+
+printf 'bead_id=%s level=INFO run_id=%s trace_id=%s scenario_id=%s phase=start seed=%s\n' \
+    "${BEAD_ID}" "${RUN_ID}" "${TRACE_ID}" "${SCENARIO_ID}" "${SEED}"
+
+start_ns="$(date +%s%N)"
+set +e
+(
+    cd "${WORKSPACE_ROOT}"
+    RUST_TEST_THREADS=1 rch exec -- cargo test -p fsqlite-wal "${TEST_FILTER}" -- --nocapture
+) >"${LOG_PATH}" 2>&1
+exit_code=$?
+set -e
+end_ns="$(date +%s%N)"
+
+duration_us="$(( (end_ns - start_ns) / 1000 ))"
+if [[ ${exit_code} -eq 0 ]]; then
+    emit_event "execute" "pilot_run" "pass" "${duration_us}" "null" true
+else
+    emit_event "execute" "pilot_run" "fail" "${duration_us}" "E_TEST_${exit_code}" true
+fi
+
+schema_conforms=true
+if ! jq -s -e '
+    all(.[]; has("trace_id")
+        and has("run_id")
+        and has("scenario_id")
+        and has("bead_id")
+        and has("core_id")
+        and has("epoch")
+        and has("lane_state_from")
+        and has("lane_state_to")
+        and has("outcome")
+        and has("duration_us")
+        and has("error_code")
+        and has("schema_conforms"))
+' "${REPORT_JSONL}" >/dev/null; then
+    schema_conforms=false
+fi
+
+summary_outcome="pass"
+summary_error="null"
+if [[ ${exit_code} -ne 0 || "${schema_conforms}" != "true" ]]; then
+    summary_outcome="fail"
+    if [[ ${exit_code} -ne 0 ]]; then
+        summary_error="E_TEST_${exit_code}"
+    else
+        summary_error="E_SCHEMA"
+    fi
+fi
+
+if [[ "${schema_conforms}" == "true" ]]; then
+    emit_event "summary" "run_summary" "${summary_outcome}" "${duration_us}" "${summary_error}" true
+else
+    emit_event "summary" "run_summary" "${summary_outcome}" "${duration_us}" "${summary_error}" false
+fi
+
+printf 'bead_id=%s level=INFO run_id=%s trace_id=%s scenario_id=%s phase=summary outcome=%s duration_us=%s path=%s replay_command=%s schema_conforms=%s\n' \
+    "${BEAD_ID}" "${RUN_ID}" "${TRACE_ID}" "${SCENARIO_ID}" "${summary_outcome}" "${duration_us}" "${REPORT_JSONL}" "${REPLAY_COMMAND}" "${schema_conforms}"
+
+if ${JSON_OUTPUT}; then
+    jq -nc \
+        --arg bead_id "${BEAD_ID}" \
+        --arg run_id "${RUN_ID}" \
+        --arg trace_id "${TRACE_ID}" \
+        --arg scenario_id "${SCENARIO_ID}" \
+        --arg outcome "${summary_outcome}" \
+        --arg report_jsonl "${REPORT_JSONL}" \
+        --arg log_path "${LOG_PATH}" \
+        --arg replay_command "${REPLAY_COMMAND}" \
+        --argjson schema_conforms "${schema_conforms}" \
+        --argjson seed "${SEED}" \
+        '{
+            bead_id: $bead_id,
+            run_id: $run_id,
+            trace_id: $trace_id,
+            scenario_id: $scenario_id,
+            outcome: $outcome,
+            schema_conforms: $schema_conforms,
+            seed: $seed,
+            report_jsonl: $report_jsonl,
+            log_path: $log_path,
+            replay_command: $replay_command
+        }'
+fi
+
+if [[ ${exit_code} -ne 0 ]]; then
+    exit "${exit_code}"
+fi
+if [[ "${schema_conforms}" != "true" ]]; then
+    exit 3
+fi
+
+exit 0
